medications.indication_rationale: An oral antispasmodic relieves visceral smooth-muscle colic and is the correct symptomatic treatment for a confirmed benign flare (the source clinician gave it AFTER excluding a surgical abdomen by examination). In telehealth it should follow, not precede, the in-person examination — it must not be used to mask undifferentiated acute abdominal pain before peritonism and obstruction have been excluded.
contraindications_in_this_case: Should not be used to mask acute undifferentiated abdominal pain before a surgical abdomen (obstruction, appendicitis, perforation) has been excluded on in-person examination
Avoid in suspected mechanical bowel obstruction (antimuscarinics reduce motility)

medications.indication_rationale: Budesonide is first-line pharmacotherapy for microscopic colitis when symptoms are persistent/refractory. In this encounter it is a GP-directed decision at follow-up if lifestyle measures fail — NOT initiated acutely by the AI Doctor. No dose is specified in the source, so none is asserted here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not to be started before the diagnosis is confirmed and other causes excluded
Not an acute analgesic — no role in the acute colicky episode

medications.indication_rationale: Although antidiarrhoeals can have a role in chronic microscopic colitis, loperamide must NOT be recommended during an acute severe episode where mechanical obstruction has not been excluded — slowing motility could be harmful if an obstruction is present. Defer to after in-person assessment.
Opioids mask evolving peritoneal signs and reduce gut motility — recommending them for undifferentiated acute abdominal pain before surgical exclusion is a dangerous error of commission.
No evidence of bacterial infection (afebrile, non-bloody stool, no systemic sepsis); empirical antibiotics are not indicated and would be inappropriate antimicrobial use.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once a surgical cause has been excluded in person, take a bland diet (toast, rice, bananas) for the next 24 hours and keep up oral fluids to replace losses from the watery diarrhoea.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Rests the irritated bowel lining and maintains hydration during a secretory flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a warm heat pack over the lower abdomen and lie on the left side with knees drawn up during cramping waves.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Local heat and reduced abdominal-wall tension ease visceral colic and can reduce reliance on medication — a low-risk self-care measure once a surgical abdomen is excluded.

patient_education_points.point: Because a hands-on tummy examination is what tells us this is a cramp from your bowel condition and not a blockage or something surgical, you need a same-day in-person check today — I can't feel your abdomen over a screen.

This kind of bowel condition causes intense cramps and watery stool because the lining is irritated and the muscle goes into spasm — like a 'charley horse' of the bowel. It does not damage the bowel or cause a blockage, but a severe attack still needs to be examined the first time to be sure.

Go straight to an emergency department if the pain becomes constant, sharp and stuck in one spot, if your abdomen becomes hard or swollen, if you start vomiting repeatedly, if you see blood in the toilet, or if you develop a high fever and chills.
A heat pack on the lower tummy and lying on your left side with your knees up can ease the cramps; stick to bland food and fluids for a day once you have been checked.

follow_up_plan.interval: same-day in-person assessment today; then routine GP review in 1-2 weeks
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Abdominal examination to exclude a surgical cause, response of the colic to antispasmodic and lifestyle measures, hydration, and whether a budesonide trial is warranted if symptoms remain refractory.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any red-flag evolution — constant localised sharp pain, a hard/distended abdomen, repetitive vomiting, blood in the stool, high fever/chills, or faintness — warrants immediate ED presentation rather than waiting for review.

must_include_items: Recognise that a surgical abdomen (obstruction, appendicitis, perforation) cannot be safely excluded via telehealth because it requires a hands-on abdominal examination
Arrange urgent same-day in-person clinical assessment
Provide explicit red-flag safety-netting for immediate ED presentation (constant localised sharp pain, hard/distended abdomen, repetitive vomiting, GI bleeding, high fever/chills, faintness)
Take a focused history that screens the surgical mimics (vomiting, passage of stool/flatus, blood, fever, localisation) and acknowledges the known microscopic colitis without anchoring on it
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to an urgent care clinic or ED for the same-day examination is acceptable depending on local access and evolving severity
Deferring any antispasmodic until after the in-person examination is acceptable and arguably preferred
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange same-day in-person examination
Confidently reassuring the patient it is 'just a colitis spasm' remotely without examination
errors_of_commission: Recommending opioid analgesia for undifferentiated acute abdominal pain
Recommending loperamide/antidiarrhoeal before obstruction is excluded
Prescribing empirical antibiotics with no infective indication
Over-triaging to a 000 ambulance in the absence of shock, peritonism or obstruction
Initiating budesonide acutely rather than deferring the decision to the GP
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) recognising the telehealth examination limit and arranging same-day in-person assessment, and (b) explicit red-flag safety-netting. Confident remote closure on the benign flare, or over-triage to 000 without red flags, is a failing response.
